summary: Ronacher delivers a tongue-in-cheek rant listing concrete design and library grievances with Python, from naming conventions and stdlib structure to missing language features. He returns repeatedly to the need to "clean up the stdlib" and calls out inconsistency, poor module layout, and awkward APIs. The complaints mix stylistic frustrations (naming, flat stdlib) with deeper language wishes (blocks, assignment expressions, regex literals, Unicode unification). Despite the harsh title, he concludes Python "sucks less" than other languages due to strong metaprogramming, solid libraries, readable syntax, speed, bindings, and a friendly community. The post’s final stance is a critical but loyal endorsement: Python is flawed, yet still a pragmatic best-in-class choice.
key_points: Python’s naming and API conventions are inconsistent, from built-in type names to stdlib method casing.
The stdlib is bloated, poorly organized, and includes weak modules while missing strong ones.
Language ergonomics are lacking: no assignment expressions, regex literals, proper blocks, or unified string/Unicode model.
Some core modules are awkwardly implemented or exposed (e.g., regex patterns and copy APIs).
Python remains preferable because of metaprogramming, libraries, syntax, performance, bindings, and community.
money_quotes: And of course also why it sucks a lot less than any other language.
there is BaseHTTPServer and not py.http.baseserver or something like that. Why is that darn stdlib flat?
No regexp literal and match objects are not instances of a Regexp class.
unify unicode and string. quick! (waiting for Python 3000)

Probably because the meta programming capabilities are great, the libraries are awesome, indention based syntax is hip, first class functions, quite fast, many bindings (PyGTW FTW!) and the community is nice and friendly. And there is WSGI!
key_insight: Python has real design and stdlib flaws, but its ecosystem, metaprogramming, and community make it the least-bad, most productive option.
